title: Do Green Jelly Beans Cause Acne? (Multiple Comparisons)

steps.instruction:
Start by reading the comic — the whole statistics lesson is in one strip.

![xkcd #882, 'Significant': after jelly beans show no link to acne, scientists test 20 colors separately; the one color that reaches p < 0.05 by chance gets a breathless newspaper headline.](https://imgs.xkcd.com/comics/significant.png)

*[xkcd #882, “Significant”](https://xkcd.com/882/) by Randall Munroe (CC BY-NC 2.5). Opens full-size in a new tab.*

gate.question: In the comic, why do the scientists keep testing one color after another?

choices.text: The overall "jelly beans" test found nothing, so they test each color hoping *some* test will come out significant
They already had strong evidence that green jelly beans were the culprit
Each color needs a larger sample than the one before it

gate.feedback: Exactly — that's the whole setup. Run enough separate tests on things that don't matter and one is bound to cross p < 0.05 by chance. The rest of this activity recreates that scenario so you can watch it happen.
gate.retryFeedback: Re-read the middle panels: the first test failed, so what did they decide to do next, and what were they hoping for?

steps.instruction: Now let's recreate the comic in this simulator. The truth is set to **H₀ is true — the treatment does nothing**, so *every* jelly bean color genuinely has **no** effect on acne. Each dot is one color's study; an **✗** means that color came out "statistically significant" (p < 0.05). Click **+1** about twenty times — one click per color.

gate.question: Out of 20 colors that truly do nothing, how many do you expect to come up "significant" at α = 0.05?

choices.text: Zero — nothing is real, so nothing should be significant

gate.feedback: Right around 1. "Significant at α = 0.05" means we accept a 5% false-alarm rate, and 5% of 20 is one. So even when nothing is going on, about one color is expected to light up by pure chance.
gate.retryFeedback: α = 0.05 is the false-alarm rate per test. Apply 5% to 20 tests.

steps.instruction: Look at the dots. Most colors came out non-significant (no ✗), but a few are marked **✗** — flagged "significant, p < 0.05." (If you didn't get one yet, click **+1** a few more times, or **+100** to see the pattern clearly.)
steps.observe: Each ✗ is a color a headline could be written about.

gate.question: A color marked ✗ was flagged "significant (p < 0.05)." Does that color actually cause acne?

choices.text: Yes — p < 0.05 proves this color is linked to acne
No — it's a false alarm; we set the truth so nothing is real, and ~5% of true-null tests reject by chance
We can't tell without a bigger sample

gate.feedback: Exactly — it's a Type I error (false alarm). We *built* this world so no color matters, yet some tests still reject H₀. That's the price of a 5% significance level: about 1 in 20 true nulls will reject on luck alone.
gate.retryFeedback: Remember how this sim is set up: every color truly does nothing. So any ✗ must be a false positive, not a real link.

steps.instruction: Now click **+100**, then **+1000**, to see the long-run false-alarm rate settle down (look at the red "Type I error" line in the results).
steps.observe: The false-alarm rate parks near **5%** — about 1 in 20 tests of a true null rejects.

gate.question: If you run 20 independent tests and each has a 5% false-alarm rate, what's the chance that AT LEAST ONE comes up significant purely by chance?

choices.text: Still about 5% — running more tests doesn't change it
About 64% — better than a coin flip

gate.feedback: About 64%. Each test has a 95% chance of NOT false-alarming, but 0.95²⁰ ≈ 0.36, so the chance that at least one of the 20 fires is 1 − 0.36 ≈ 0.64. With 20 shots at significance, finding a "hit" is more likely than not — even when nothing is real.
gate.retryFeedback: The chance NONE of the 20 false-alarms is 0.95²⁰ ≈ 0.36. So the chance at least one does is 1 minus that.

steps.instruction: The newspaper ran *"Green Jelly Beans Linked to Acne (p < 0.05)!"* — but the scientists had quietly run all 20 color tests and reported only the one that hit.

gate.question: What is the core problem with that headline?

choices.text: Nothing — the result was significant at p < 0.05, so it's valid
It spotlights the one significant test while hiding the 19 that weren't — and with 20 tries, one false positive was expected
The sample size was too small to publish

gate.feedback: Yes — this is the multiple-comparisons ("look-elsewhere") problem. Cherry-picking the single significant result out of 20 and burying the rest turns an expected false positive into a fake discovery. The p-value of the "winner" is meaningless once you ignore how many tests it took to find it.
gate.retryFeedback: Count the tests that were run versus the number reported. What got left out, and was a hit expected anyway?

steps.instruction: One defense is a **stricter threshold**. Change **Significance level (α)** to **0.01**, click **↺** to reset, and run **+1000** again.
steps.observe: The false-alarm rate drops to about **1%** — far fewer colors light up by chance.

gate.question: Why does lowering α help when you're running many tests?

choices.text: A stricter threshold makes each individual false alarm rarer, so a batch of tests produces fewer of them
It increases the power to detect real effects
It has no effect on the false-alarm rate

gate.feedback: Right. This is the idea behind a Bonferroni correction: if you plan to run 20 tests and want an overall 5% false-alarm rate, test each one at 0.05 ÷ 20 = 0.0025 instead of 0.05. Stricter per-test thresholds keep the *whole batch's* error rate in check (at the cost of some power).
gate.retryFeedback: Lowering α moves the bar for calling any single test "significant." What does that do to the number of false alarms across many tests?

steps.reveal: Run enough tests on things that don't matter and chance alone hands you a "significant" result — the headline writes itself. This is the **look-elsewhere effect**, and it's why a lone p < 0.05 is weak evidence when you don't know how many tests were run behind it. The real defenses: **decide your hypothesis in advance** (pre-registration), **correct α for the number of tests** (Bonferroni or false-discovery-rate methods), and **replicate** before believing. A finding that survives a fresh, pre-planned test is worth far more than the best of 20 fishing expeditions.
